title: CART: Context-Aware Terrain Adaptation using Temporal Sequence Selection for Legged Robots
abstract: 自然界中的动物通过视觉与触觉等多模态感知来认知地形，并形成在不平坦地形上稳定行走的理解。同样地，足式机器人需要建立视觉与本体感知之间的关联认知，才能提升在复杂地形中的稳定行走能力。当前多数地形适应方法依赖先验经验——特别是视觉传感器的观测数据，导致其在非结构化越野地形中容易失效。这种基于经验的学习方式常引发"视觉-纹理悖论"，即视觉观测与触觉感知之间产生认知偏差。本研究提出CART高层控制器，该控制器采用情境感知式地形适应方法，通过整合机载传感器的本体感知与外感受信息，实现鲁棒的地形认知能力。我们在IsaacSim仿真器中利用ANYmal-C机器人开展多地形测试，并在波士顿动力SPOT机器人上完成实物实验。为评估习得的情境地形特征，我们创新性地采用机器人基座振动稳定性作为度量指标。仿真与实物实验均表明，与配备多模态感知的先进基线方法相比，CART在仿真环境中的平均成功率提升5%，实物实验中综合稳定性分别提升45%和24%，且未增加机器人完成运动任务的时间消耗。
html_content:
CART: Context-Aware Terrain Adaptation using Temporal Sequence Selection for Legged Robots
Kartikeya Singh, Youngjin Kim, Yash Turkar, and Karthik Dantu
University at Buffalo
Corresponding author:
ksingh35@buffalo.edu
Abstract
Animals in nature combine multiple modalities, such as sight and feel, to perceive terrain and develop an understanding of how to walk on uneven terrain in a stable manner.
Similarly, legged robots need to develop their ability to stably walk on complex terrains by developing an understanding of the relationship between vision and proprioception. Most current terrain adaptation methods are susceptible to failure on complex, off-road terrain as they rely on prior experience, particularly observations from a vision sensor. This experience-based learning often creates a Visual-Texture Paradox between what has been seen and how it actually feels. In this work, we introduce
CART
, a high-level controller built on a context-aware terrain adaptation approach that integrates proprioception and exteroception from onboard sensing to achieve a robust understanding of terrain. We evaluate our method on multiple terrains using an ANYmal-C robot on the IsaacSim simulator and a Boston Dynamics SPOT robot for our real-world experiments. To evaluate the learned contextual terrain properties, we adapt vibrational stability on the base of the robot as a metric. We compare
CART
with various state-of-the-art baselines equipped with multimodal sensing in both simulation and the real world.
CART
achieves an average success rate improvement of
5%
over all baselines in simulation and improves the overall stability up to
45
% and
24
% in the real world without increasing the time taken by the robot to accomplish locomotion tasks.
I
INTRODUCTION
Autonomous navigation of legged robots through diverse and complicated environments has gained attention over the past few years to perform critical tasks such as payload delivery
[
13
]
, search and rescue
[
3
]
, environmental inspection
[
23
]
, precision agriculture
[
27
]
, and others. Legged robots such as Boston Dynamics’ SPOT
[
10
]
, Unitree Go1/Go2
[
38
]
, and Anybotics ANYmal
[
19
]
provide basic legged locomotion, enabling them to walk on different terrains. The primary challenge in using them for off-road navigation is to incorporate a detailed understanding of the terrain and use this for high-level planning and control for safe and stable locomotion.
Several previous approaches identify rich semantic information of an off-road terrain using images
[
40
]
,
[
17
]
,
[
48
]
,
[
47
]
. These methods are capable of identifying semantic class distributions across distinct off-road environments but are not robust to illumination and viewpoint changes. To overcome these issues, other works make use of LiDAR point clouds
[
39
]
,
[
9
]
that remain unperturbed in varying lighting conditions. Additionally, diffusion using various high-level sensors
[
21
]
,
[
15
]
results in a more detailed representation of the surfaces. These exteroception-based approaches are robust to the segmentations of fine-grained terrains but do not account for the control/planning and stable locomotion of a robot (specifically, legged robots) on these segmented classes.
Sim-to-real approaches
[
1
]
,
[
14
]
have been explored in establishing twin policies to make the robot learn
Appearance
and
Tactility
about the terrain type explicitly. However, these approaches are tightly coupled with the simulated representations they acquire for training (such as scan-dots in
[
1
]
and privileged scanning in
[
26
]
). These simulated experience-based learning approaches are well crafted to adapt the physical attributes such as agility, traction control, or dexterity to perform control and coordination tasks, but lack adaptation to real-world nuances such as the
Visual-Texture Paradox
. This discrepancy between visual and sensory artifacts could lead to overly conservative and further unstable locomotion. In this work, we present a unified learning paradigm for legged robots that integrates exteroceptive and proprioceptive sensory modalities to enhance contextual understanding between visual perception and physical interaction. We further utilize the contexts established by
CART
to develop a high-level control policy prioritizing the robot’s stability on complex terrains.
Figure 1
:
Context switching using
CART
:
Robot traverse through slushy snow overlayed on grass. On the left, we show the actual image projection from the robot’s perspective that describes the raw image and snow-grass segments using
[
40
]
. We show a
Visual-Texture paradox
instance between vision only
[
12
]
and
CART
’s estimated context using vision and proprioception.
Some prior works
[
31
]
,
[
42
]
,
[
44
]
have relied solely on exteroception to plan and navigate legged robots through complex environments. However, methods using proprioception (velocity/hip positions) from the robot’s locomotion, along with exteroception from distinct perception sensors (camera/lidar), have also been used for terrain adaptation
[
11
]
,
[
43
]
,
[
1
]
, depicting the stable locomotion robustness. These approaches not only estimate the terrain representation but also adapt to stable locomotion by using proprioceptive signals like hip positions and efforts, highlighting the crucial role of proprioception in maintaining robot stability
[
11
]
. We build
CART
using the same motivation while using proprioceptive signals as the reward function to account for vibrational stability. A more detailed related survey of our adapted stability metric is mentioned in
subsection
II-B
.
One of the instances depicting a visual-texture paradox scenario has been shown in
Figure 1
. Our primary observation is that the stability of the robot is related to the nature of the terrain model is learning, which can only be determined by establishing a context between the visual appearance of the terrain and how it is physically felt by the robot when it traverses that terrain. In this work, we present
CART
, a context-aware locomotion strategy that is capable of robust terrain understanding with minimal dependency on pre-trained terrain attributes and uses it to improve a robot’s stability in complex, unseen environments.
CART
does so by selecting the most context-aware sequences to determine robot gait, i.e., its velocity and walk height. The work most closely related to ours is
[
6
]
, which highlights the importance of proprioceptive signals over additional privileged inputs for terrain understanding. However, exteroceptive sensing becomes necessary when predicting risk along a forward horizon to enable risk-aware locomotion. Unlike single-modality approaches, CART dynamically shifts its contextual focus between exteroception and proprioception, leveraging each modality when it is most informative for terrain assessment.
The main contributions of this work are as follows:
•
We introduce
CART
, a learning-based controller that explicitly incorporates terrain type (using camera-based exteroception) and how the robot ”feels” when it navigates over that terrain to design the control policy.
•
From our evaluations,
CART
improves the vibrational stability of a legged robot by
45
% in simulation and
24
% in our real world without affecting the overall traversal time. Because of this,
CART
also achieves
5%
higher success rate in completing the navigation task in simulated environments while traversing extremely complex terrains.
Figure 2
:
Overview of the Pipeline:

inputs a stream of RGBD images

S_{v}
, friction meshes

, and proprioception data

(joints torques & velocity, and feet slips) resulting in a state space

. We train a modular high-level locomotion policy

with an added attention-based context vector
C

that determines the context between the exteroception and proprioception using our defined rewards. We formulate a reward function that minimizes the velocity tracking, proprioceptive slip (offset of hips and legs from the base), and joint efforts for energy. Next, we perform policy gradient inference on
θ
\theta
using our Temporal Sequence Selection module that selects the varying length sequences during the inference and unwraps the command velocity and height as the final outputs. The trained and current observations are matched and scored in the TSS module to determine the optimal sequence to be selected during the inference. This matching of the segments in the library
𝒮

\mathcal{S}_{\theta}
helps in solving the visual-texture paradox (when the learned theta (

) doesn’t match with the current

p

, proprioception only feedback)
II
Related Work
II-A
Terrain Estimation Using Sensing
Legged robots’ navigation capability has been validated
[
46
]
,
[
1
]
across complex, uneven, and varying terrain while ensuring safety and efficiency. With the hardware advancements in the industry, these robots have not been left untouched from performing some of the extreme navigation tasks like
[
45
]
,
[
14
]
. Perpetual observation of terrain is a vital step in making robust and collision-free navigation of a robot successful. Sensor-based perception using exteroception has been exploited over the years, which could result in a high-level but coarse characterization of a specific terrain.
Leveraging sensor-based perception works like
[
17
]
, and
[
32
]
utilizes images to determine risk-aware path planning for a legged robot. However, these methods, without any additional signal, result in a lack of internal feedback and could make the robot’s body unaware of its current state. To overcome these issues,
[
11
]
couples both exteroceptive and proprioceptive parameters of a robot to adapt between different gaits. Similarly, RL-based methods like
[
24
]
,
[
1
]
generalize over different types of legged robots by adapting the various privileged information like simulated terrain profile, friction coefficients, and robot proprioception, but they are not well acquainted with the real-world terrain representations because of the sim-to-real distinction.
II-B
Vibration damping for sensors on robotic platforms
Legged robots like Boston Dynamics’ Spot and ANYMAL-C are designed to traverse complex, uneven terrain. However, these highly actuated robots are susceptible to creating intense, high-frequency motion jitteriness at the robot’s base (pitch, roll, yaw). Tightly coupled sensors like camera/LiDAR inherit vibrations occurring on the base of the robot, leading to severe image blur or point cloud distortion
[
7
]
, inefficient localization and mapping
[
28
]
[
36
]
and descriptor matching
[
16
]
. An entire domain of research in image and video stabilization is dedicated to addressing these issues.
[
2
]
presents a stabilized vision system by using a fast pan and tilt unit (PTU) on the fully torque-controlled hydraulically actuated quadruped robot (HyQ) to compensate for the vibrations, impacts, or slippages on rough terrain. On the other hand,
[
41
]
tackles the problem of SLAM degradation due to vibrations in the coupled sensors in rugged terrains by proposing a hybrid gimbal system with virtual suspension control and terrain-adaptive planning. Therefore, our work stabilizes the vibrations on the robot’s base to compensate for vibrations occurring in the coupled sensors, like cameras/LiDAR mounted on the robot.
II-C
Learning-based High-Level Controller for Legged Robots
Model-based control approaches integrate an articulated dynamics robot model by leveraging physics and bio-inspired modeling techniques
[
4
]
,
[
20
]
. However, the constrained range of motion and non-adaptability in the robot’s state bring suboptimal outcomes in robust-legged locomotion. The rise of deep reinforcement learning and the hardware evolution of legged robots has been complemented by simulation software like MuJoCo
[
37
]
, PyBullet
[
5
]
, and Isaac Sim
[
29
]
. These simulations are capable of providing real-world-like environments that can be used to model different high-level controllers for a legged robot. A typical Markov Decision Process (MDP) can be obtained using the privileged sensory-based observation directly from a simulator. These observations include multimodal sensing and proprioceptive sensors (Joint positions, velocity, and effort) that could be used to formulate a reward function for policy training of legged robot locomotion, including Proximal policy optimization (PPO) algorithms
[
33
]
, Soft actor-critic (SAC)
[
18
]
), and student-teacher (S&T)
[
26
]
. These handcrafted policies have shown improvements in learning stable locomotion on varying terrains. However, experience-based learning often fails to optimally estimate the true nature of the terrain due to the out-of-distribution terrain properties.
III
Method
III-A
Overview and Notion of Stability
CART consists of two components: (i) a context-aware high-level command policy and (ii) a Temporal Sequence Selection (TSS) module (Fig. 2). CART outputs
base-level
commands that are executed by an underlying low-level locomotion controller (Spot’s built-in controller on hardware and a learned low-level controller in simulation). The low-level controller is treated as a black box that converts commanded base motion into joint-level actuation.
Vibrational stability.
In this work, “stability” refers to
vibrational stability
on the robot base: reducing oscillatory disturbances induced by foot–ground interaction that propagate to the base and can degrade onboard sensing (camera/depth) and perception-action coupling. This is distinct from whole-body locomotion stability in the classical sense.

This compact representation is used both for high-level command generation and for TSS context scoring.
III-C
Hierarchical Execution: High-Level Outputs vs Low-Level Controller
A key design choice in CART is compatibility with off-the-shelf legged platforms whose joint-level controllers are proprietary or otherwise inaccessible. CART therefore operates in a hierarchical manner:
High-level (CART) outputs.
CART outputs base-level commands:

Low-level execution.
The underlying low-level locomotion controller consumes
(

)

and produces joint-level actuation (e.g., desired joint positions/torques) to track the commanded base motion and height. On Spot, this mapping is performed internally by the built-in controller; in simulation, the same interface is provided by a learned low-level controller. CART is therefore controller-agnostic: it can be deployed on different robots and low-level controllers as long as a standard base-command interface is available.
III-D
Loss Function for Vibrational Stability
We define a differentiable stability-oriented objective computed from the commanded outputs and measured signals, and optimize the network parameters to increase this objective. The objective balances: (i) motion efficiency (via a velocity-tracking shaping term to avoid trivially slow commands), (ii) slip minimization via
Δ

\Delta q
(lateral leg offsets during stance), (iii) effort regularization, and (iv) command smoothness.

III-E
Temporal Sequence Selection (TSS)
TSS operates on a
pre-trained
base command policy and selects parameter subsequences that best match the current context. Importantly, TSS selects
parameter subsequences
(not entirely separate policies). This selection is performed online during inference
Figure 2
to handle a mismatch between the learned and current context.

1

and use the selected subsequence to modulate high-level command generation. Concretely, we temporarily apply the selected parameter subsequence within the high-level policy and compute the commanded base velocities and height for execution by the low-level controller, after which the original parameters are restored. This enables context-aware adaptation without retraining the base command policy.
Runtime feasibility.
The context scoring and selection are executed online at the same frequency as the robot interface. For a sequence library with
132
,
775
132{,}775
segments, the scoring and selection run at approximately 400 ms per action in our implementation, demonstrating practical feasibility for deployment.
IV
Results and Discussion
To evaluate the terrain adaptation acquired from
CART
, we simulate the proposed locomotion strategy
θ
∗
\theta^{*}
in the IsaacSim simulator using the ANYmal robot and validate it in the real world using Boston Dynamics’ Spot robot. The proposed policy is robust to handle marginal model discrepancy by solely updating the proprioceptive dimensions from one robot to another, which motivates us to use two different robotic platforms to demonstrate the fundamental advantages.
IV-A
Simulation Experiment Setup
Figure 3
:
IsaacSim terrain configurations used for training and testing of all baselines along with
CART
during our experiments. The top and bottom rows represent the difficulty of the same terrain type used for training and testing.

Our simulated experiments were carried out on Isaacsim’s legged environment
[
29
]
using an Anybotics ANYmal-C robot. We exploit the terrain configuration flexibility IsaacLab provides to design multiple deterministic terrains based on the difficulty. To evaluate the performance of the proposed method, we designed four different simulation environments
Figure 3
using the IsaacSim simulator: (1) Box, (2) Rough, (3) Downward slope, and (4) Upward slope. The first two simulation environments were designed to demonstrate maneuverability on a flat, rugged surface, while the latter two were for elevated terrain. The training scheme included easier terrain difficulty, and all baselines were trained on them and tested on harder terrains as shown in
Figure 3
.
Furthermore, we evaluate CART by characterizing publicly available baselines into two domains:
Proprioceptive Feedback (p)
and
Proprioceptive with Exteroceptive Feedback (p+e)
methods for high-level command velocities and height prediction methods. We compare
CART
with various baselines on the IsaacSim simulator using an ANYmal robot, using the following baselines:
Blind (p)
[
8
]
,
[
34
]
,
[
35
]
: The IsaacSim provides a blind policy framework to train using low-level controllers for stable locomotion of various quadruped robots. This policy solely relies on high torque-based joint motions but lacks exteroception.
PPO (p+e)
[
30
]
: A Proximal Policy Optimization policy that leverages terrain features using an onboard height scanner as privileged information from the IsaacSim simulator, along with proprioception (joint positions, velocities, and torques).
S&T (p+e)
[
26
]
: State-of-the-art student-teacher policy that efficiently combines exteroceptive features using privileged sensing and proprioception using an onboard sensing system.
IV-B
Real-world Experiment Setup
Figure 5
:
Real-world experiment scenario with multiple rugged terrains and elevations, including: Grass, Mud, Concrete, Gravel, and Mulch. The experiments were conducted over various underlying terrains, such as Grass over mud and Grass over Gravel. The red markers represent the waypoints resulting in 7 runs per baseline.
Our real-world experiments were conducted using a Boston Dynamics’ Spot robot on various rugged terrains with varying elevations as shown in
Figure 5
. We follow the same point-to-point navigation task schema as the simulation. We compare the performance of
CART
with Spot’s against the following baselines:
Spot’s Inbuilt (p+e)
[
10
]
: The internal feedback system of Spot is equipped with an advanced stability mechanism to monitor and regulate the acceleration and stability of the robot. The real-time feedback loop from the robot’s internal software helps it to adjust the 3d acceleration even during a manual input of velocities. In our evaluations, we compare
CART
with three different walking modes from the spot’s inbuilt controller:
TROT
,
CRAWL
, and
AMBLE
using identical commanded velocities.
VAPOR (p+e)
[
43
]
: Risk-aware stable navigation method that computes the robot’s velocity using exeroception and proprioception to maintain a stable locomotion in real-world unstructured environments. VAPOR provides optimal robot
candidate actions and velocity commands using its proposed stability vector. We utilize their state outputs from their planner and compare the stability performance against
CART
.
To ensure consistency in our evaluation experiments, identical average speeds were used to evaluate the stability performance of the robot using various walking styles (TROT, CRAWL, and AMBLE) and
CART
. Additionally, we evaluate a terrain-aware stability improvement model
[
43
]
using its velocity space restriction module, designed for real-world environments, against
CART
using the same identical average velocities. We compare CART with various baselines in the real-world setting by adapting 7 different off-road scenarios as shown in
Figure 5
.
We experienced a lack of publicly available baselines that are agnostic to the legged robot, as they remain highly distinct in their proprioceptive parameters, as shown in
[
25
]
. Re-implementing the baselines from simulation to the real world requires proprietary software kits such as
[
22
]
that could be complex and expensive. Therefore, we compare
CART
against robot-specific locomotion baselines for simulation and real-world.

Success rates:
CART
achieve better success rates during the simulation experiments when compared with existing baselines.
Data Collection and Setup:
Data for real-world training was collected across different terrains like grass, concrete, gravel, pebble sidewalks, etc. Whereas, the IsaacSim model was trained using terrain configurations shown in
Figure 3
. During the data collection for both simulation and real-world, we recorded High-volume data from the Spot’s and ANYmal’s joints (position and effort), hips (position and velocity), feet (slips), robot’s command velocity, and height. We used a Zed2i camera mounted on top of Spot’s robot to collect RGBD images and a Microstrain IMU for the body oscillation evaluation. The data collected from the real world exhibited distinct visual characteristics like grass, gravel, mud, etc. However, the simulation terrain lacked this property and relied only on depth and proprioception. Therefore,
CART
scores the selected sequences using proprioceptive and depth context only. This property of
CART
couples the contexts from other terrains that the robot might acquire while walking.

IV-C
Evaluation Metrics and Results
Success Rates:
The success rates of the ANYmal robot while traversing through unseen and complex terrains in IsaacSim. The corresponding simulation results with 100 trials per terrain are illustrated in
Figure 6
. The locomotive tasks were defined to make the robot follow an intended path from one point to another on each given terrain. Since the source and goal were pre-defined, we evaluate the time taken by the robot to complete tasks in
Table I
. We report the mean of
10 successful experiments, because the blind policy performed very poorly.
Experimental Performance Analysis:
In order to account for the efficiency of the
CART
, we analyze various parameters by measuring task completion time, total distance traveled, mean jerk, and vibrational disturbances. The simulated test runs were performed using predefined paths that the robot was intended to follow. However, in the real world, the planner was missing. Therefore, we calculate the total distance traveled by the robot on a particular terrain of long horizon in a given time. As used in
[
43
,
11
]
, we adapt the vibration cost that determines the overall offset of the hip joints from the body as a metric. While testing our approach on varying terrains, we extract the joint offsets (positions) from
𝐏
t
∈
ℝ
d
P
\mathbf{P}_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{P}}
. The corresponding evaluation metrics are tabulated in
Table I
. Our experiments demonstrate better performance in both simulation and real-world implementation across all the compared baselines. However, it is noted that in the real-world experiment, Spot’s inbuilt CRAWL mode experienced the least amount of jerk as it damps the robot’s height to the minimum. This results in substantially shorter distances covered within the constrained time limit, which may not be ideal for task-oriented maneuver scenarios.

Testing base vibration:
To analyze how foot lateral foot daviations (
Δ
​
q
\Delta q
) influence base stability we performed a controlled kinematic study using the same spot urdf and an inverse kinematics gait gnerator as shown in
Figure 4
.
We evaluate the stability by minimizing the body orientation vibrations that could result in optimized image and video stabilization as explained in
subsection
II-B
. To evaluate this, we mount an additional IMU at the center of the robot body to measure its orientation (Yaw, Pitch, and Roll) and rate of change, which permits us to implicitly evaluate the vibrational stability of the robot. The IMU readings demonstrate overall orientation and rate perturbation with respect to its equilibrium points. The corresponding stability measurements are provided in
Table II
and
Table III
, simulation and real-world, respectively. As demonstrated, the proposed method outperforms across various terrain types and baseline algorithms.

V
Conclusion and Future Work
We present
CART
, a context-aware terrain adaptation method for robust and stable navigation for quadruped robots in irregular and complex terrains. Our approach incorporates the robot’s exteroception and proprioception to estimate the desired velocity and height. Our approach not only accounts for the stability of the robot while navigating on irregular terrains but also adapts to real-world nuances such as the visual-texture paradox to accurately predict the pseudo-terrain properties. Our learning framework incorporates the visual representations of specific terrain and creates a correspondence with the appropriate velocity and height required for specific terrain. We evaluate our method in terms of the notion of the robot’s stability and simulation-based success rates with an improvement up to
5
%. We observe our method to acquire more stability up to an average of
45
% in simulation and
24
% in the real world when tested on various unseen irregular terrains with varying elevations.
The modular approach presented in this work is not limited to predicting high-level states suitable for the robot to traverse complex terrains. The Temporal Sequence Selection Framework (TSS) module presented in this work provides the flexibility to unwrap many other parameters acquired by the robot while training, such as low-level torque, body orientation, COM projection, etc. These parameters could be utilized for adaptive path planning, semantics-based reversibility analysis for mobile robots, and energy-aware navigation.

preview_text:
Animals in nature combine multiple modalities, such as sight and feel, to perceive terrain and develop an understanding of how to walk on uneven terrain in a stable manner. Similarly, legged robots need to develop their ability to stably walk on complex terrains by developing an understanding of the relationship between vision and proprioception. Most current terrain adaptation methods are susceptible to failure on complex, off-road terrain as they rely on prior experience, particularly observations from a vision sensor. This experience-based learning often creates a Visual-Texture Paradox between what has been seen and how it actually feels. In this work, we introduce CART, a high-level controller built on a context-aware terrain adaptation approach that integrates proprioception and exteroception from onboard sensing to achieve a robust understanding of terrain. We evaluate our method on multiple terrains using an ANYmal-C robot on the IsaacSim simulator and a Boston Dynamics SPOT robot for our real-world experiments. To evaluate the learned contextual terrain properties, we adapt vibrational stability on the base of the robot as a metric. We compare CART with various state-of-the-art baselines equipped with multimodal sensing in both simulation and the real world. CART achieves an average success rate improvement of 5% over all baselines in simulation and improves the overall stability up to 45% and 24% in the real world without increasing the time taken by the robot to accomplish locomotion tasks.

CART: Context-Aware Terrain Adaptation using Temporal Sequence Selection for Legged Robots
Kartikeya Singh, Youngjin Kim, Yash Turkar, and Karthik Dantu
University at Buffalo
Corresponding author:
ksingh35@buffalo.edu
Abstract
Animals in nature combine multiple modalities, such as sight and feel, to perceive terrain and develop an understanding of how to walk on uneven terrain in a stable manner.
Similarly, legged robots need to develop their ability to stably walk on
one_line_summary: 该论文提出一种基于上下文感知的地形适应方法，通过时序序列选择提升四足机器人在复杂地形上的稳定性，但未涉及强化学习、VLA、扩散模型等核心关键词。
